Dear Mr.
Lowenstein:
I am writing to request guidance from the commission on three
matters related to my work before the state Energy Resources
Conservation and Development Commission.
(1)
I currently serve on two advisory committees to the
Energy Commission
One advisory Committee on "intermittent
ignition devices" was established pursuant to Public Resources Code
Section 25951 which requires the Commission to "develop in coopera»
tion with affected industry and consumer representatives, who will
be designated as such representatives by the Commission, the
specifications
for certification of intermittent ignition devices."
The SeCDnd advisory committee on which
I now serve is Charged with
the duty of developing building conservation standards for non—
residential construction pursuant to Health and Safety Code sece
tions 19375 et
seq.
Pursuant to Commission order,
that Committee
now includes: We: of"special consultants"
who serve
in addition
‘20 the SCECUtOX‘Y members.
I
starve as one Of the special Consultantsi
It is my understanding that the FPFC has tentatively decided
that participation in such advisory committees does not create
reporting obligations pursuant to the lobbyist provisions of the
Fair Political Practices Act.
I would like to request formally,
however,
the Current views of the FPPC on this matter.
12)
1 would like to inquire whether my participation on either
of these Committees brings me within the "public official"
category
of the Act, and therefore places lobbyist reporting obligations on
others who may discuss with me the subjects covered in either of
these committees.
"Earn
mm, 1mm! n,”


The Honorable Daniel Lowenstein
June
23y
1576
page two
(3)
1f the answer to ﬂy second question is affirmative to
any extent,
I would like to know if "in-mouse" discussions with
other staff members at NRDC concerning subject matter relevant
to either committee could possibly subject those individuals to
reporting requirements under the Actr
Finally,
I enclose a completed lobbyist registration statement
for myself in keeping with NRDC'S policy as enunciated in John
Leshy's letter to you in July of last year.
It appears that
under the current definition of lobbying under the Act, the
lobbyist reporting provisions of the Act do not cover my activities

a
lobbyist registration statement.
A lobbyist activity
authorization has previously been filed by NRDC.
Based on my conclusion that my advisory committee activities
are not subject to the reporting requirements of the Act,
I am

sections.question: 1) Does participation in advisory committees to the Energy Commission create reporting obligations pursuant to the lobbyist provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act? 2) Does participation on these committees bring the person within the "public official" category, placing lobbyist reporting obligations on others who discuss committee subjects? 3) Could "in-house" discussions with other staff members concerning committee subject matter subject those individuals to reporting requirements?
sections.conclusion: Participation in purely advisory committees does not make one a lobbyist. Whether advisory committee service creates "agency official" status depends on whether the boards have decision-making authority. The Commission provides standards from Opinion No. 73-099 regarding the Citizen's Advisory Committee of the Bay Conservation and Development Commission to help determine if boards are advisory or decision-making. If the boards are purely advisory, there are no reporting obligations for board members or their employers, and persons discussing committee matters are not subject to lobbyist reporting.
sections.question_synthetic: A Natural Resources Defense Council staff member serving on advisory committees to the State Energy Commission seeks guidance on whether such service creates lobbyist reporting obligations for themselves or others who discuss committee matters with them.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that service on purely advisory committees does not trigger lobbyist status, and provides a test based on prior Commission opinions to determine whether committees are truly advisory versus decision-making bodies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of three letters: incoming request dated June 23, 1975; interim response dated July 2, 1976; and final FPPC response dated August 11, 1976. The incoming request provides context; extraction focused on the August 11 FPPC response letter. Some OCR quality issues with document scans but core content clearly legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Does participation in advisory committees to the Energy Commission create reporting obligations pursuant to the lobbyist provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act? 2) Does participation on these committees bring the person within the "public official" category, placing lobbyist reporting obligations on others who discuss committee subjects? 3) Could "in-house" discussions with other staff members concerning committee subject matter subject those individuals to reporting requirements?

CONCLUSION: Participation in purely advisory committees does not make one a lobbyist. Whether advisory committee service creates "agency official" status depends on whether the boards have decision-making authority. The Commission provides standards from Opinion No. 73-099 regarding the Citizen's Advisory Committee of the Bay Conservation and Development Commission to help determine if boards are advisory or decision-making. If the boards are purely advisory, there are no reporting obligations for board members or their employers, and persons discussing committee matters are not subject to lobbyist reporting.

embedding.summary: This file contains correspondence between Richard Cotton of the Natural Resources Defense Council and the FPPC regarding whether his service on advisory committees to the State Energy Commission triggers lobbyist reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC ultimately advises that purely advisory committee membership does not create reporting obligations.
